revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23993722414|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Jōnan-gū]][[ja:城南宮]] [[co:commons:Category:Jōnan-gū]][[ja:城南宮]] [[co:commons:Category:Jōnan-gū]][[ja:城南宮]] [[ja:城南宮]] { { { {{Infobox religious building| |Name = Jonangu Shrine |image = [[File:Jonangu shrine.jpg|250px]]
Jonangu Shrine Torii {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=250|coord={{coord|34|57|4|N|135|44|47.6|E}}}} |Location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}{{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto City|1=Kyoto}}{{ill|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|en|Fushimi-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1197771|lt=Fushimi Ward|1=Fushimi-ku, Kyoto}} Nakajima, Tobahirikyū-machi 7 |Coordinates = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|57|4.03|N|135|44|47.6|E|region:JP-26_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined Deities = Jonan Daijin ({{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|lt=Yachihōshin|lt_ja=八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Ikinagatabihironoson|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kunichōritonoson|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}) |Shrine Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社|1=Shikinaisha|2=ja|3=式内社|1=Shikinaisha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikinaisha|13=User:Immanuelle|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|lt=Shikinaisha}} (minor; within the precincts, subsidiary shrine: {{ill|Mahata-sunjinja|ja|真幡寸神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mahata-sunjinja|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}})
Formerly a {{ill|Fusha|ja|府社|1=Fu-sha|qid=Q134917326}}{{efn|lt=Fu-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fusha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q712534}} |Founding = Unknown |Main Shrine Building = {{ill|Nagare-zukuri|en|Nagare-zukuri|qid=Q210684|lt=Ryuzō|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |Other inscriptions = {{ill|Shinbutsu Reijō Junsai no Michi|ja|神仏霊場巡拝の道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu Reijō Junsai no Michi|1=Sacred Sites of Kamigata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10444949}} No. 83 (Kyoto No. 3) |Annual Festival = July 20 }} [[File:Jonangu Kyoto15n3200.jpg|thumb|240px| Praye r Hall'''Jonangu Shrine''' (城南宮) is a Shinto shrine located in Fushimi Ward, Kyoto City. Its former shrine Rank was that of a Fusha, and it is known as the "Great Shrine of Hōjo." The subsidiary shrine, '''Mahata-sunjinja''', is classified as a Shikinaisha. == Enshrined Deities == * Principal Deity – Jonan Daijin ({{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|lt=Yachihōshin|lt_ja=八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Ikinagatabihironoson|1=Empress Jingū}}, {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kunichōritonoson|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}}) == History == The founding date is unknown. It is said to have been established during the transfer of the capital to Heian-kyō, when {{ill|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|en|Kuni-no-Tokotachi|qid=Q478504|lt=Kuninotokotachi-no-Mikoto|1=Kuni-no-Tokotachi}} was enshrined together with {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|lt=Yachihōshin|lt_ja=八千矛神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} and {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Ikinagatabihironoson|1=Empress Jingū}}. Because the shrine is located to the south of the city (the former capital), it was called "Jonan Shrine." It is also said to have been founded as the guardian shrine (chinju-sha) of Jōnan-ji.{{Cite book|和書|title=京都大辞典|date=1984-11-12|year=1984|publisher=株式会社淡交社|page=504}} After Emperor Shirakawa constructed the Toba Rikyu (Jonan Rikyu) as part of his detached palace, the shrine became integrated into that complex and, as the guardian shrine of the palace, received frequent visits from successive emperors and retired emperors. In later years it came to be regarded as the deity protecting the rear (ura) of the demon gate (kimon) of Kyoto Imperial Palace. In 1221 (Shōkyū 3), Retired Emperor Go-Toba held a yabusame event at Jonangu Shrine. This served as a pretext for gathering forces to overthrow Hojo Yoshitoki; although the retired emperor instigated the Jōkyū War, he was ultimately defeated by the advancing Kamakura shogunate.{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.nikkei.com/article/DGXZQOIH07B370X00C21A4000000/ |title=承久の乱800th year 　追討令一転「武士の世」促す |publisher=日本経済新聞 |date=2021-04-15 |accessdate=2022-05-21}} Also, at that time, during the popular pilgrimages to the Kumano Sanzan, Jonangu served as a purification station to ensure safe travel, a tradition that has evolved into the current practice of repelling evil.{{Cite book|和書|title=歴史散歩26 京都府の歴史散歩　中|date=2011-08-30|year=2011|publisher=株式会社山川出版社|pages=246–247}} Sometime during the Muromachi period, it appears that the Mahata-sunjinja that once stood at this location was incorporated into Jonangu Shrine. Although the shrine was devastated during conflicts such as the Ōnin War, it was restored during the Edo period. In Bunkyū 3 (1863), Emperor Kōmei visited the shrine for a jōi prayer, which led to the custom of holding representative prayer ceremonies three times a year (at New Year, May, and September). Furthermore, this site was the main battlefield during the Battle of Toba-Fushimi in Keiō 4 (1868), when the old shogunate forces collapsed before the "Nishiki no Mihata" banner of the new government.{{Cite book|和書|title=日本歴史地名大系第二十七巻　京都市の地名|date=1979-09-20|year=1979|publisher=株式会社平凡社|pages=432–433}} In 1876 (Meiji 10), it was classified as a Shikinaisha under the name "Mahata-sunjinja" and renamed as such, and it was listed as a Fusha. However, in 1968 (Shōwa 43) the name was reverted to "Jonangu Shrine," with Mahata-sunjinja being reestablished within its precincts as a subsidiary shrine with a new building. In 1977 (Shōwa 52) the main shrine building burned down, but it was rebuilt in 1978 (Shōwa 53). In recent years, Jonangu Shrine has been widely venerated not only as a talisman for safe relocation and travel but also as a guardian of traffic safety; every July, a ritual known as the "Kayanowa-guguri" (passing through a straw ring) is held for automobiles. == Shrine Grounds == * Main Shrine Building – Rebuilt in 1978 (Shōwa 53) * Front Shrine (Haiden) * Connecting Corridor (Tsubasadorō) * Inari Shrine * Itsukushima Shrine, Sumiyoshi Shrine, and Hyojin Shrine * Awashima Shrine * Tenmangu Shrine, Myōken Shrine, and Kotohira Shrine * Kōshin Shrine * Ōkuninushi Shrine * Kasuga Shrine * Worship Hall (Haiden) * Kagura Hall * Shinkōsha * Musubi Hall * Traffic Safety Prayer Hall * Ritual Hall (Saikan) * Purification Fountain (Chōzuya) – Where the water known as "Kikusui Wakami" flows * Sanshō Shrine * Ema Hall * Serikawa Tenmangu – Also known as Karatodotenmangu * Shrine Office * Reception Hall * Peripheral Shrines ** Asukada Shrine File:Jonangu Kyoto01n3200.jpg|Stone Torii File:Jonangu Kyoto20n3200.jpg|Main Shrine File:Jonangu Kyoto18n3200.jpg|Worship Hall File:Jonangu Kyoto16n3200.jpg|Kagura Hall File:Jonangu Kyoto14n4272.jpg|Mikoshi House File:Jonangu Kyoto07n3200.jpg|Ema Hall File:Jonangu Kyoto06n3200.jpg|Karatodotenmangu File:Jonangu Kyoto12n4272.jpg|Sanshō Shrine File:Jônan-gû Shintô Shrine - Miko no Mai.jpg|Miko Dance at Kagura Hall === Mahata-sunjinja === [[File:Jonangu Kyoto03n3200.jpg|thumb|Sample image|Mahata-sunjinja]] '''Mahata-sunjinja''' (まはたきじんじゃ) – Enshrined deities: Mahata-sun Daijin and {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Ojin Tennō|1=Emperor Ōjin}}. It is traditionally said that this Shikinaisha originally stood in the Fukakusa area and was founded in the 3rd year of the regency of {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Jingū-kōgō|1=Empress Jingū}} (203), when, during her campaign in the Three Han, a banner was raised on board a ship with the divine spirit of Jingū-kōgō and the deity of {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōkuninushi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大国主}} (as represented by Yachihōshin) accompanying it. In Eikyō 10 (1438), the Inari shrine that once stood atop Inariyama in Kyoto Prefecture was relocated to the foothills at Fuji-o (present-day Fushimi Inari Taisha), and the shrine there, known as Fuji-o-sha, was moved to Fujimori (present-day Fujimori Shrine). Thus, it is said that Mahata-sunjinja, originally located in the Fujimori area, was transferred to its present location. It later appears to have become integrated with Jonangu Shrine, and a new Mahata-sunjinja was constructed in 1968 (Shōwa 43). === Shinen "Rakusuien" === The garden was designed by {{ill|Kinsaku_Nakane|en|Kinsaku_Nakane|qid=Q11366237|lt=Nakane Kinsaku|1=Kinsaku Nakane}}. * Garden "Hana no Niwa" – features a formation known as "Spring Mountain." * Garden "Heian no Niwa" – a pond-strolling garden; the annual event "Kyokusui no Utage" is held here in spring (April 29) and autumn (November 3). * Muromachi Garden – a pond-strolling garden. * Momoyama Garden * Jonan Detached Palace Garden File:Jonangu Kyoto28n4272.jpg|Heian no Niwa (Heian Garden) File:Kyokusui no utage.jpg|Kyokusui no Utage at Heian no Niwa File:Jonangu Shinen Japanese apricot.jpg|Japanese apricot in full bloom in spring File:Jonangu Kyoto38n4272.jpg|Muromachi Garden File:Jonangu Kyoto41n4272.jpg|Momoyama Garden File:Jonangu Kyoto48n4272.jpg|Jonan Detached Palace Garden === Jonangu Shrine Torii === The red torii facing the main shrine building is of an unusual style. Known as the Jonangu Shrine Torii, its basic form belongs to the Shinmei torii type; however, it features manju (dumpling-like ornaments) at the base of the pillars, with shimagigi and kasagi arranged atop the ridge, and even a roof is installed. In the center of the shimagigi, a metal fitting bearing a divine crest is affixed. == Towano Kakehashi == On National Route 1 adjacent to Jonangu Shrine, a pedestrian overpass known as "Towano Kakehashi" was erected from 1965 (Shōwa 40) until 2010 (Heisei 22). This structure was privately funded by Jonangu Shrine to ensure the safety of local pedestrians and pilgrims, and was managed as a road occupancy facility by the shrine. Due to reduced usage following the installation of a crosswalk at a nearby intersection and due to aging, its removal was decided; Jonangu Shrine bore the full cost of removal and contracted the Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism to carry out the demolition.{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/kyoto/contents/pdf/22-1112-1.pdf |title=Removal of the dilapidated "Towano Kakehashi" (National Route 1 in front of Jonangu Shrine) due to aging|publisher=[[Kinki Regional Development Bureau]] Kyoto National Highway Office|date=2010-11-12 |accessdate=2021-12-24 |archiveurl=https://web.archive.org/web/20130129030450/http://www.kkr.mlit.go.jp/kyoto/contents/pdf/22-1112-1.pdf |archivedate=2013-01-29 }} == Transportation == * {{ill|Takeda_Station_(Kyoto)|en|Takeda_Station_(Kyoto)|qid=Q1952588|lt=Takeda Station|1=Takeda Station (Kyoto)}} (on the Kintetsu Kyoto Line and Kyoto Municipal Subway Karasuma Line) – approximately a 15-minute walk == Footnotes == {{Reflist}} == Related Articles == {{Commonscat}} {{Osm box|w|285894524}} * {{ill|Kujuukuoji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujuukuoji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} – Jonangu Shrine is regarded as the departure point from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q740246}} for Kujuukuoji. * {{ill|Jonangu-dō|ja|城南宮道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jonangu-dō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Shin Jonangu-dō|ja|新城南宮道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin Jonangu-dō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * {{ill|Oseki Mochi|ja|おせき餅|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oseki Mochi|1=Q115483255|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q115483255}} == External Links == * [http://www.jonangu.com Jonangu Shrine] (Official site) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Pref-stub|pref=京都府}} ==References== {{reflist}} {{Ninety-Nine Ōji}} {{translated page|ja|城南宮|version=102721644|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:城南宮]] [[ja:郡戸王子]] [[zh:郡戸王子]] {{Commons category|Jōnan-gū}} [[ja:城南宮]] [[ja:城南宮]] {{wikidata link|Q11426034}} [[Category:Buildings and structures in Fushimi-ku, Kyoto]] Yamashiro Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to Empress Jingū]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]]
